Why This Institute Exists

1,400 years of drift.

When Moses left his people for 40 days, they built an idol and started worshipping it. God recorded that in Qur'an 2:51 — not as ancient history, but as a warning for every community that follows.

Now think about 1,400 years. Political dynasties needing obedient populations. Clergy paid to maintain the status quo. Hadith compiled 200 years after the Prophet died by people who never met him. Zoroastrian rituals rebranded as Islam. Scholars elevated to lords.

The Qur'an calls itself complete, detailed, and sufficient. It was the first thing they abandoned.

— The Central Question

We started this institute because we read the Qur'an and couldn't find most of what we were told was mandatory. So we went back to the source — the Arabic text, the root words, the historical record. What we found changed everything.

"And He lays corruption upon those who do not reason."
Qur'an 10:100
Based on Dr. Sayed Abdul Wadud's Research

How the Original Message Was
Systematically Replaced

The drift from the Qur'an wasn't accidental. We found it happened through specific, documented processes — verifiable in history and confirmed by the Qur'anic text itself.

01
Conspiracy One

God replaced with human intermediaries

The direct relationship between a person and God was broken by inserting human middlemen. Messengers became objects of worship. Priests became required go-betweens. Pirs, saints, Imams, and scholars became the new authorities. The Qur'an is clear: sovereignty belongs to God alone and no human stands between you and your Creator.

"They have taken their scholars and priests as lords besides God." — 9:31
02
Conspiracy Two

Islam split into sects from within

A two-pronged operation: first, political weakening through manufactured sectarian division. Second, ideological corruption through fabricated hadith, false interpretation, and infiltration from Persian, Jewish, and Zoroastrian traditions. The result: hundreds of sects each claiming exclusive truth, none returning to the Book that started it all.

"Those who divided their religion into sects — you are not of them in anything." — 6:159
03
Conspiracy Three

The Qur'anic text itself was attacked

Groups promoted the idea that the present Qur'an is corrupted, that its words carry hidden meanings only Imams can access, and that the real text was suppressed. The Qur'an demolishes every version of this claim. God personally guaranteed its preservation (15:9). The Prophet confirmed its completeness before he died.

"Indeed, We have sent down the Reminder, and We will surely preserve it." — 15:9
04
Conspiracy Four

Sufism replaced action with passivity

Sufism entered Muslim civilization wearing Islamic terminology, but its roots are in Greek philosophy, Neo-Platonism, and Hindu mysticism. It replaced the Qur'an's call to establish justice and reform society with passive inner experience and devotion to saints. One of the most effective tools ever used to neutralize Islamic social reform.

"Do you order people to righteousness while you forget yourselves?" — 2:44
05
Conspiracy Five

Namaaz replaced Salat

Based on Aurangzaib Yousufzai's research and confirmed through Lane's Arabic Lexicon: the word "Salat" (root: ص-ل-و) means to follow closely, pursue, remain attached — a complete system of conduct. The five-times ritual called Namaaz has roots in pre-Islamic Zoroastrian practice. It was institutionalized by the Umayyad dynasty as a control mechanism. The Prophet established Salat. What most Muslims practice today is Namaaz. They are not the same.

"Establish salat — salat prevents indecency and wrongdoing." — 29:45
06
Conspiracy Six

Human sovereignty replaced God's

The Umayyad dynasty imported the Christian concept of "divine right of kings" into Islam, calling rulers "shadows of God on earth." Democracy, autocracy, theocracy, and clergy-run systems all share the same flaw: they replace God's sovereignty with human sovereignty. The Qur'an says the command belongs to God alone, exercised through His Book — not through dynasties or man-made constitutions.

"The command is for none but God." — 12:40
Moses left for 40 days. His people built an idol. What happened over 1,400 years?
Qur'an 2:51
Our Core Research Findings

What We Found When We
Actually Read the Qur'an

Not fringe positions. These come directly from the Qur'anic text. We checked every verse, traced every root word, and followed the evidence wherever it led — even when it was uncomfortable.

1
Salat — What It Really Means

Salat is not ritual prayer. It is a complete system of conduct.

We studied all 102 occurrences of "salat" in the Qur'an. Not one describes specific physical postures, specific daily timings, or specific Arabic phrases to recite. The Arabic root (ص-ل-و) means to follow closely, to pursue, to remain attached — confirmed in Lane's Lexicon, the definitive classical Arabic dictionary. Salat describes how you conduct your life according to divine guidance. Not a five-minute physical exercise five times a day.

"Establish salat — salat prevents indecency and wrongdoing." — 29:45
2
Hadith — The Hard Evidence

The Qur'an names hadith as the problem — using that exact word.

The Qur'an calls itself complete (6:115), detailed (6:114), sufficient (29:51), and preserved (15:9). It warns against following "other hadith" — using that exact Arabic word — in 7:185, 45:6, and 77:50. The first four Caliphs (Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali) all refused to allow hadith to be written. Bukhari was born 194 years after the Prophet. This is documented, undisputed history.

"In what hadith after this will they believe?" — 77:50
3
Reasoning — The Obligation

The Qur'an makes critical thinking a religious obligation. Repeatedly.

The word "aql" — intellect and reason — appears 49 times. "Tafakkur" — deep reflection — appears 18 times. The Qur'an asks "do you not see?" and "do you not think?" dozens of times. It condemns following forefathers blindly in 2:170 and 5:104. It says those who do not reason are worse than cattle in 8:22. The book most Muslims claim to follow commands the one thing most Muslim institutions punish: independent critical thought.

"Indeed, the worst of creatures are those who do not reason." — 8:22
4
Ramadan — The Actual Command

Ramadan was about engaging the Qur'an — not restricting food.

The Qur'an introduces Ramadan with: "The month in which the Qur'an was revealed as guidance for mankind" (2:185). The purpose is engagement with that revelation. "Siyam" means purposeful restraint and focused attention. In 19:26, Mary observed siyam as a vow of silence — while she ate and drank. The food-fasting institution replaced the actual command: deep, sustained engagement with the Book revealed in that month.

"The month of Ramadan in which the Qur'an was revealed as guidance." — 2:185
5
Masjid — Function, Not Structure

A masjid is defined by what it does — not what it looks like.

The Qur'an says masjids can be used to cause harm (9:107). It says people are blocked from entering them (2:114). It says they belong to God alone (72:18). It mentions birds performing salat. None of this makes any sense if masjid means a building and salat means a prayer ritual. The Qur'an consistently defines both by their purpose and function — not by architecture, carpets, or minarets.

"The masjids belong to God, so do not invoke anyone alongside God." — 72:18
6
Shahada — What's Actually in the Text

The Qur'anic testimony contains only one name: God's.

The Qur'an presents exactly one testimony: "La ilaha illa Allah" — there is no god but God. That's 3:18, and it's God Himself taking that testimony along with the angels and those with knowledge. Adding "Muhammadur rasulullah" as a required paired statement has no Qur'anic basis. Archaeological evidence shows the dual shahada evolved as a political tool of the Umayyad dynasty. We have a 22-point case built entirely from Qur'anic verses proving this.

"God bears witness that there is no deity except Him." — 3:18
"When it is said to them, follow what God has revealed — they say: We will follow what we found our fathers doing."
Qur'an 2:170
Dr. Sayed Abdul Wadud

Quranocracy:
The Governance the Qur'an Actually Prescribes

This is one of the most important and least discussed arguments in Islamic thought. Dr. Wadud's book Quranocracy makes the case that none of the governance systems humanity has tried — democracy, autocracy, theocracy, bureaucracy — can solve human problems, because they all share one fatal flaw: they place sovereignty in human hands.

The Qur'an prescribes something entirely different: a system where the immutable principles of the Qur'an form the constitutional foundation, while human consultation (Shura) handles the practical details within those boundaries. Not clerical rule. Not popular vote. Not personal decree. Sovereignty of the Book.

"The command is for none but God — He has commanded that you accept the subservience of none but Him."

— Qur'an 12:40

Key Arguments from Wadud's Research

The Sovereignty Problem
Democracy places sovereignty in the people — but the Qur'an says sovereignty belongs to God alone
Democracy's basic premise is that the will of the majority determines right and wrong. The Qur'an says "the command is for none but God" (12:40). These two positions cannot be reconciled. Pakistan's democratic failures, Wadud argues, are not implementation problems — they are a fundamental incompatibility between the system and what God prescribed.
Qur'an 12:40 · 6:57 · 5:44
The Khalifa Question
Man is not God's "vicegerent" — he is a successor to previous generations
The common translation of "khalifa" as "vicegerent of God" is linguistically wrong and theologically dangerous. The Qur'an uses khalifa to mean successor — one who comes after another in their absence. God is never absent. The concept of "delegation of God's powers" to rulers or clergy has no Qur'anic basis — it was invented to justify autocratic rule. Abu Bakr himself refused to be called "khalifatullah" (successor of God), insisting only on "khalifat rasulillah" (successor to the Messenger).
Qur'an 2:30 · 7:69 · 10:14
Permanent vs Changeable
Qur'anic principles are immutable — their application in law can change with time
A Quranocratic constitution has two layers: permanent Qur'anic principles (justice, equality, no usury, Shura governance, protection of life and property) that cannot be changed by any legislature — and practical by-laws determined through mutual consultation (Shura) that can evolve with changing circumstances. Fiqh laws by medieval Imams were always meant to be changeable by-laws, not permanent religious rulings. Treating them as permanent is a major source of Islamic stagnation.
Qur'an 6:115 · 42:38 · 5:3
The Ahadis Position
Hadith and Fiqh laws have no place in an Islamic constitution
Wadud documents that even within Pakistan's constitution, the phrase "Quran and Sunnah" is used decoratively — with actual governance remaining entirely secular. More critically, the definition of "Sunnah" in Article 227 is left to each sect to interpret independently, meaning the constitution formally endorses sectarian division. The only solution: the Qur'an alone as the constitutional foundation, with Hadith and Fiqh removed from their false position as religious law.
Qur'an 6:38 · 5:3 · 15:9
Universalism vs Nationalism
The Qur'an's political goal is universal brotherhood — not nation-states
Nationalism divides humanity into competing groups. Internationalism merely manages that competition. The Qur'an prescribes something fundamentally different: Universalism — one code of life for all humanity, regardless of race, language, color, or national origin. "Mankind was one single nation" (2:213). The Qur'anic vision is a world united not by force but by submission to the same divine principles of justice, equality, and human dignity.
Qur'an 2:213 · 10:19 · 49:13
Hadith Refutations

15 Arguments for Hadith.
15 Qur'anic Answers.

Every standard argument made for hadith authority — answered directly from the Qur'an. Not our interpretation. The actual text.

"Most of them follow nothing but conjecture. And conjecture can never replace the truth."

— Qur'an 10:36

Bukhari was born 194 years after the Prophet. Muslim was born 206 years after. These men compiled oral chains spanning multiple generations. The Qur'an asks: "Do not follow what you have no knowledge of" (17:36).

01
The Argument
"Obeying the Messenger means following Hadith"
The Qur'an defines what obeying the Messenger means: obeying the revelation he delivered. The Prophet was himself commanded to follow only what was revealed to him (6:50). His duty was to convey — not to create a second religious source. No verse equates obeying the Messenger with obeying books compiled two centuries after his death.
Qur'an 24:54 · 6:50 · 5:99
02
The Argument
"The Messenger was sent to explain the Qur'an — so we need Hadith for his explanations"
The word in 16:44 is "li-tubayyina" — to make evident, not to add external content. The Qur'an states its verses are perfected (11:1) and that it clarifies itself (25:33). The Prophet's role was to deliver and live by the Qur'an — not produce a parallel source alongside it.
Qur'an 16:44 · 25:33 · 11:1
03
The Argument
"Hadith provides the details of Salat and Zakat the Qur'an doesn't specify"
"We did not leave anything out of the Book" (6:38). When you understand Salat through its actual Arabic root — not through post-Qur'anic ritual assumptions — the Qur'an is entirely sufficient. "This Book is a clarification for all things" (16:89).
Qur'an 6:38 · 29:45 · 16:89
04
The Argument
"The Qur'an is too general — Hadith fills in the necessary specifics"
The Qur'an calls itself "mufassal" — fully detailed (41:3). It calls itself "tibyan" — clarification for all things (16:89). God asks directly: "Is it not sufficient for them that We sent down the Book?" (29:51). If God says it is sufficient, claiming it needs supplementing directly contradicts His own word.
Qur'an 29:51 · 41:3 · 6:114
05
The Argument
"God preserved Hadith as part of preserving the religion"
God promises to preserve exactly one thing: "Indeed, We have sent down the Reminder, and We will surely preserve it" (15:9). The Reminder is the Qur'an. No other source has this divine protection. Hadith is a human compilation — God never guaranteed its accuracy or preservation.
Qur'an 15:9
06
The Argument
"The Prophet had independent legal authority beyond the Qur'an"
The Prophet himself said: "I do not follow except what is revealed to me" (6:50). He judged only by God's revelation (5:48). The Qur'an warns him directly against following personal desires or tradition when making judgments (5:49). His authority came from the Qur'an — nothing else.
Qur'an 6:50 · 5:48 · 5:49
07
The Argument
"The companions followed Hadith — so should we"
The Qur'an explicitly rejects this reasoning: "They followed their forefathers even if they understood nothing" (2:170). "If you obey most of those on Earth, they will mislead you from God's path" (6:116). Truth is not determined by historical precedent or community practice — it is determined by what God revealed.
Qur'an 2:170 · 6:116
08
The Argument
"Rejecting Hadith means rejecting the Prophet himself"
"The Messenger's duty is only to convey clearly" (5:99). Accepting the Prophet's mission means accepting the Qur'an he conveyed. Verse 59:7 — misused constantly for this argument — specifically addresses distribution of war spoils in a particular historical situation. It is not a general mandate for hadith authority.
Qur'an 5:99 · 59:7
09
The Argument
"Isnad science verifies which Hadith are authentic"
Chains of oral narrators spanning 200 years remain human conjecture — not divine preservation. "Do not follow what you have no knowledge of" (17:36). "They only follow conjecture, and conjecture is no substitute for truth" (53:28). No chain of human memory equals a divine guarantee. The Qur'an demands knowledge — not probability assessments.
Qur'an 17:36 · 53:28
10
The Argument
"Hadith fills the gaps the Qur'an left open"
"We did not leave anything out of the Book" (6:38). "Today I have completed your system for you" (5:3). God declares the system complete. Claiming there are gaps contradicts His own declaration. You cannot simultaneously believe 5:3 and claim the Qur'an is insufficient.
Qur'an 6:38 · 5:3
11
The Argument
"Sunnah is a second form of divine revelation"
53:3-4 refers to the Qur'an being recited — not personal sayings. The Prophet stated he had no knowledge of the unseen and followed only revelation (6:50). If Sunnah were revelation, God would have preserved it the same way He preserved the Qur'an. He did not. That absence is itself the evidence.
Qur'an 53:3–4 · 6:50 · 15:9
12
The Argument
"The Prophet's legal judgments prove law exists outside the Qur'an"
"So judge between them by what God has sent down" (5:48). "Do not follow their desires over the truth that has come to you" (5:49). The Qur'an commands the Prophet to judge only by revelation and warns him directly against using tradition or personal preference. Every judgment was Qur'anic in origin.
Qur'an 5:48 · 5:49
13
The Argument
"Scholarly consensus (Ijma) confirms Hadith authority"
"If you follow most of those on Earth, they will mislead you from God's path" (6:116). "They have taken their scholars and priests as lords besides God" (9:31). The Qur'an doesn't just fail to support this — it directly and explicitly warns against exactly this kind of reasoning.
Qur'an 6:116 · 9:31
14
The Argument
"The same people who preserved the Qur'an preserved the Hadith"
"Indeed, We have sent down the Reminder, and We will surely preserve it" (15:9). God preserves the Qur'an — not human transmitters. The Qur'an never equates human sincerity with divine preservation. These are categorically different claims and the Qur'an treats them as such.
Qur'an 15:9
15
The Argument
"You simply cannot practice Islam without Hadith"
"This Qur'an guides to that which is most upright" (17:9). "We have sent the Book as clarification for all things" (16:89). "Hold fast to what We have revealed to you" (43:43). The Qur'an declares itself practical, complete, and sufficient for every generation. Claiming otherwise contradicts God's own words about His own Book.
Qur'an 17:9 · 16:89 · 43:43
"Most of them follow nothing but assumption. And assumption can never replace the truth."
Qur'an 10:36

About This Institute

Not tradition.
Not ritual.
Understanding.

We're based out of Columbus, Ohio. This institute started because we read the Qur'an and couldn't find most of what we were told was mandatory. So we studied the Arabic. Traced every root word. Read Wadud, Yousufzai, Siraj Islam, and Joseph Islam's research. Went through the historical record on hadith compilation, Umayyad political history, and the origins of traditional Islamic practice.

What we found wasn't surprising once we saw it clearly: most of what is practiced as Islam today was constructed 200 to 300 years after the Prophet died, by people with political and financial interests in replacing the Qur'an's authority with their own.

Every claim we make is backed by a specific verse. Every argument can be tested against the Qur'anic text. We don't ask you to take our word for it — we ask you to read the Qur'an.
